text:
The ABC is under fire after sending a radio duo to the Paris Olympic Games at the vast expense of the taxpayer despite the pair failing to provide commentary on any sporting events.

An ABC radio duo, whose trip to Olympic Games cost taxpayers $135,000, failed to comment on any sporting events while in Paris, it has been claimed.

A Senate hearing heard taxpayers funded Adelaide hosts Sonya Feldhoff and Jules Schiller’s trip to the French capital to the tune of $135,000.

The ABC said the pair were to provide “additional reporting on the ground and present the local breakfast show every day” during their sponsored work trip.

But Feldhoff and Schiller did not present the network’s listeners with any sports commentary during the Games, according to the Adelaide Advertiser.

Instagram posts from the duo show the pair instead kicked an AFL football with members of a French team, met with a Moulin Rouge dancer, taught fans to cheer ‘Aussie, Aussie, Aussie, Oi, Oi, Oi’ and conducted vox pop interviews.

“When I mention the word Australia to you, what do you immediately think of?” Feldhoff could be heard asking tourists in an interview.

She and Schiller also imitated drinking water from France’s polluted River Seine and compared the city’s streets to those in Australia.

“Mmmm … delicious,” Schiller said of the infamous river water.

He said ABC Adelaide’s recording studio in the city, referred to by the team as ‘studio de Triomphe’, “looks like a hotel room”.

It’s understood the pair’s $135,000 of taxpayer money went towards flights, accommodation, hospitality, equipment, transport and broadcasting costs.

An ABC spokesperson revealed to SkyNews.com.au Feldhoff and Schiller travelled to Paris via economy class and said the pair "added significantly to the ABC’s coverage".

"Sonya and Jules are experienced broadcasters who added significantly to the ABC’s coverage across our local radio network and other ABC platforms throughout the Olympic games. This included delivering their live Breakfast program daily, interviews with athletes and key Olympic figures and almost 60 live crosses to other ABC local radio stations," the spokesperson said.

"The ABC contracted the rights to broadcast the Olympics across regional and remote Australia as well a number of capital city ABC stations, including Adelaide.

"All travel and accommodation was within ABC travel guidelines. Flights for all members of the team who travelled from ABC Radio Adelaide were economy class."

It comes after the national broadcaster said the duo had a “French adventure” and “immersed themselves in all the action at street level to make us feel like we were part of the action”.

This prompted South Australian Liberal Senator Alex Antic to lash out against the broadcaster for allowing taxpayers to fund their lavish trip, despite allegedly failing to fulfil their duties.

“It is staggering that the ABC thinks that spending $135,000 to send two breakfast radio hosts and two staff to Paris for the Olympic games represents value for money for the Australian taxpayer,” Mr Antic said.

“It is difficult to reconcile spending like this with repetitious claims that the ABC is underfunded,” he said.

It comes after ABC’s own chairman Kim Williams took aim at the broadcaster’s priorities and slammed its handling of stories in July.

“Australians are growing tired of being told they have to tighten their belts when our public broadcaster isn’t prepared to do the same,” he told an address to Radio National staff.

“We need to have a better coherent logic in relation to story prioritisation.

“And I think that's not surprising, and in fact, is much more responsive to the way in which people expect new services to be delivered.

“I think I make no apology for the fact I think news should be prioritised appropriately.

“Sorry if that's unsatisfying.”

It follows ABC's star presenters Paul Barry and Laura Tingle recently becoming the subject of full throttled rebukes for insinuating the criticism of Kamala Harris was rooted in her gender and heritage and labelling Opposition Leader Peter Dutton "dangerous" while called Australia a racist country, respectively.
